content.text: A risk-based feasibility gate is a structured checkpoint that a use case must pass before it is eligible to receive development funding. Without gates, investment decisions rely on the enthusiasm of the sponsoring team rather than an objective assessment of risk. Use cases that fail on technical feasibility, data readiness, business case, or regulatory compliance receive funding, enter the build phase, and are killed months later at significantly greater cost. The gate process converts feasibility assessment outputs into a binary investment decision, with documented evidence to support it.

attrs.alt: Four-gate feasibility process: Quick Screen, Feasibility, Business Case, Ethics/Compliance

content.text: The standard waterfall error is to fund discovery and build in a single step, then assess risk once development is underway. By the time a team has spent four months building a use case, the organisational cost of killing it — sunk cost bias, team morale, political capital — makes the kill decision almost impossible regardless of what the risk assessment shows. Gates before funding are cheap: they cost a few days of analysis. Gates after funding are expensive: they cost months of rework or an unacknowledged write-off.

The function of a feasibility gate is not to block investment — it is to ensure that investment decisions are made with explicit risk awareness rather than optimistic assumptions. A use case that passes all four gates is still uncertain; a gate simply documents what is known and unknown at the time of the decision.

— Takes 2–4 hours. Eliminates use cases with obvious misalignment or hard blockers before any detailed analysis begins. Saves the feasibility assessment effort for use cases that have a realistic chance of proceeding.
— Takes 1–2 weeks of analysis. Combines technical, data, and skills feasibility scores into a composite confidence score. Produces a go/no-go recommendation for the business case analysis investment.

— Takes 1–2 weeks. Requires a risk-adjusted financial model (NPV, payback period, sensitivity analysis). Ensures that the use case's expected value justifies the development investment even in bear scenarios.

— Takes 1–4 weeks depending on regulatory complexity. Legal, compliance, and ethics review before any customer data is processed or any automated decision reaches a production environment. A compliance failure in production is orders of magnitude more expensive than one caught at the gate.

Gate 1 is designed to be fast and binary. It asks two questions: does the use case align with the organisation's GenAI strategy, and are there any hard blockers that make the use case non-starters regardless of technical or financial merit?
Clearly maps to a named strategic priority (cost reduction, customer NPS, regulatory compliance, new product)
Indirect alignment — benefits a strategic priority but is not explicitly named in strategy
No clear connection to organisational strategy
Legal question raised but not yet answered — legal review required before Gate 4
Known legal prohibition (e.g. automated financial decision without required explanation right under GDPR Art. 22)
Named sponsor at VP level or above with committed funding if gate passes
Sponsor identified but budget not yet allocated
No existing system or approved use case covers the same capability
Partial overlap with existing system — scope must be clarified
Exact duplicate of an already-approved or already-deployed use case

Gate 1 Kill outputs go to the portfolio backlog with a documented reason. If strategic priorities change or blockers are resolved (e.g. a legal question is answered favourably), the use case can be resubmitted without repeating the full feasibility analysis.

Gate 2 aggregates the outputs of the technical feasibility assessment and the data readiness assessment into a composite score. The composite weights reflect that infrastructure and data are roughly equally important for most GenAI patterns, with skills as a modifier:
≥ 2.0 (hard minimum — below this is a hard kill regardless of composite)
≥ 2.0 (hard minimum — below this is a hard kill)
No hard minimum; < 2.0 adds conditional requirements
≥ 3.0 to proceed; 3.0–3.49 = Conditional Pass with documented mitigations
— Proceed to Gate 3 business case analysis.
— Proceed with documented infrastructure/skills gap log. Each gap must have a named owner, a resolution date, and a mitigation plan if the gap is not resolved by the start of the build phase.

— Address gaps and resubmit. Common defers: gateway not deployed (addresses tech score); labelled evaluation set not yet created (addresses data score); hiring in progress for required ML engineer (addresses skills score).

— The use case depends on infrastructure or data that is fundamentally not available. A kill at Gate 2 is not permanent: resolving the underlying gap (building a gateway, establishing data access) makes the use case eligible for resubmission.

Gate 3 evaluates whether the use case's financial case justifies the investment risk. The thresholds below are indicative — organisations should calibrate them against their hurdle rate and the risk profile of the specific use case:
Use bear scenario NPV, not base case. If bear NPV is negative, the use case does not have a robust business case.
GenAI infrastructure costs typically frontloaded. Payback > 24 months indicates high cost relative to benefit or slow adoption ramp.
If the ROI model only turns positive at > 40% adoption, the use case is fragile — small adoption shortfalls destroy the business case.
No single benefit > 70% of total NPV
A use case whose NPV is 85% attributable to one benefit (e.g. FTE reduction) is highly sensitive to that assumption. Concentrated benefits require additional sensitivity testing.

A use case that passes Gate 3 thresholds in the base scenario but fails in the bear scenario should not be funded without explicit sponsorship sign-off acknowledging the downside risk. The gate process is not a guarantee that the use case will succeed — it is a record that the risk was understood at the time of the decision.

Gate 4 is the final checkpoint before funding approval. It covers regulatory compliance, algorithmic fairness, and ethical use of AI. Unlike the earlier gates, which are primarily analytical, Gate 4 requires formal sign-off from legal, compliance, and (where applicable) the organisation's AI ethics board or risk committee.
Credit scoring, claims settlement, employment decisions
GDPR Art. 22 requires human review right; must be built into the product before go-live. Legal sign-off required.
Insurance pricing, mortgage advice, investment recommendations
FCA / PRA rules on model risk, explainability, and customer outcomes. Requires Risk function sign-off.
Clinical decision support, triage, diagnostic assistance
MDR/MHRA (UK) or FDA (US) classification may apply. Regulatory pathway must be confirmed before build.
Equality Act 2010 (UK) / Title VII (US) prohibits discriminatory automated decisions. Bias audit required.
Consumer protection, advertising standards, data protection. DPA, opt-out mechanisms, content moderation required.
For use cases where the model's output affects individuals or groups differently, a bias assessment must be completed before Gate 4 can pass. The assessment covers:
— Does the training data, grounding corpus, or input feature set contain proxies for protected characteristics (age, gender, ethnicity, disability, religion)? If so, how are these handled?

— Run the evaluation set disaggregated by relevant subgroups. If accuracy, false positive rate, or false negative rate differs significantly (>5 percentage points) between subgroups, document the disparity and the remediation plan.
— If the model's outputs are used to generate future training data (e.g. 'adjuster accepted the recommendation' is used as a label), does the model risk amplifying existing biases over time?

— Is there a clear mechanism for affected individuals or the operational team to override the model's output? Automated decisions without a human review option are high-risk under most regulatory frameworks.

A Gate 4 CONDITIONAL PASS with documented compliance conditions is not a sign that the use case is high-risk — it is a sign that the governance process is working. Most production use cases will have at least one compliance condition (a DPA to sign, a human review step to implement, an audit log to build). The condition is tracked through to go-live, not closed at funding.
Fund and schedule for build phase
Fund with conditions tracked to go-live; sponsor acknowledges conditions
Conditions must be closed before deployment sign-off, not before funding
Use case has resolvable gaps — not ready now
Return to backlog with gap log; sponsor reviews in 3–6 months
Gaps closed, scores re-assessed, re-submitted to the relevant gate
Use case has unresolvable blocker or insufficient value
Archived with reason documented; does not return to backlog without fundamental change
New submission required if circumstances change (strategy shifts, legal opinion changes, market changes)
The gate process requires a defined review committee with quorum rules. Without a committee, gate decisions are informal and non-binding — individual teams can override them without accountability.
— Product owner + CoE lead. Can be completed asynchronously via a checklist submission. Turnaround: 2–3 business days.
— CoE lead + platform engineering lead + data engineering lead. In-person or synchronous review of the feasibility assessment outputs. Turnaround: 5–7 business days after assessment is submitted.
— Finance sponsor + CoE lead + executive sponsor. Finance must validate the ROI model inputs independently before the gate meeting. Turnaround: 5–10 business days.

— Legal counsel + compliance officer + data protection officer (if personal data involved) + AI ethics lead (if role exists). Turnaround: 10–20 business days depending on regulatory complexity; legal review may require external counsel for novel use cases.

Risk-based feasibility gates are structured checkpoints that convert discovery assessments into binary investment decisions. Gates before funding are cheap (days of analysis); gates after funding are expensive (months of rework).

Gate 1 (Quick Screen) filters for strategic alignment and hard blockers. Gate 2 (Feasibility) combines technical, data, and skills feasibility into a composite score. Gate 3 (Business Case) requires a risk-adjusted NPV above the hurdle rate with acceptable payback period. Gate 4 (Ethics/Compliance) requires formal legal and compliance sign-off.

Kill outcomes at any gate are not permanent for most cases — they are invitations to resolve the underlying gap and resubmit. The gate log documents the reasons, so future resubmissions don't repeat the same analysis.

Conditional Pass outcomes carry tracked conditions through to go-live. Compliance conditions (DPA, human review step, audit log) are not closed at funding — they are dependencies for the deployment sign-off.
Each gate requires a defined review committee with quorum rules. Informal gate reviews without accountability lead to gate theatre: the process exists but does not influence decisions.
